responses.text: The 2018 Odyssey Online article describes 'Birds Aren't Real' as a growing movement at the College of Charleston, where students claim that biological birds were replaced by government surveillance drones starting in 1959.

While the article characterizes the movement as a potentially 'satirical phenomenon,' it reflects a time when some observers were unsure if local participants were promoting a serious conspiracy or sharing an inside joke.
In reality, 'Birds Aren't Real' is a widespread satirical conspiracy theory created by Peter McIndoe in January 2017.
The movement's lore claims that the U.S. government systematically killed off all biological birds in the mid-20th century and replaced them with drone replicas to spy on citizens.
The phenomenon began spontaneously as an impromptu joke when McIndoe wrote the slogan on a sign during a local demonstration in Memphis.

Since its inception, the parody has spread to college campuses nationwide, spawning local chapters, physical rallies, and merchandise sales as a Gen Z performance art piece intended to spoof actual misinformation.

McIndoe stayed in character as a dedicated conspiracy theorist for years, only breaking character in late 2021 to publicly confirm the movement was an elaborate parody of the modern conspiracy theory landscape.
